2026-03-30T10:37:34.8068834Z {"timestamp":"2026-03-30T10:33:55.22542965Z","direction":"IN","type":"RESPONSE","server_id":"github","payload":{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"content":[{"text":"{\"number\":37864,\"title\":\"Tracking: Known pre-existing CI test failures\",\"body\":\"## Purpose\\n\\nThis issue tracks **known, pre-existing CI test failures** that are caused by infrastructure, service-side, or external dependency issues — not by code bugs in this repository. The automated [Analyze CI Test Failures](.github/workflows/fix-test-failures.md) workflow uses this issue to skip known failures and avoid filing duplicate issues.\\n\\n## Known Failures\\n\\n_No known CI (playback) failures at this time._\\n\\n\\u0026gt; **Note:** This list only tracks failures from **CI pipelines** (`ci.yml`, playback mode). Live-test pipeline failures (`tests.yml`) are excluded — the workflow filters them out by check-run name.\\n\\n## Maintenance Guidelines\\n\\n- **Adding entries:** When a CI failure is confirmed to be a pre-existing infrastructure/service issue (not a code bug), add it here with the package name, test pattern, error summary, root cause, and a tracking issue link.\\n- **Removing entries:** When the underlying issue is fixed and CI is green again, remove the entry and close the corresponding tracking issue.\\n- **Format:** Each entry must include the **package name** (as it appears in check-run names) so the workflow can match it.\\n- **Review cadence:** Review this list monthly to check if any known failures have been resolved.\\n\",\"state\":\"open\",\"html_url\":\"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/issues/37864\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"deyaaeldeen\",\"id\":6074665,\"profile_url\":\"https://github.com/deyaaeldeen\",\"avatar_url\":\"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6074665?v=4\"},\"author_association\":\"MEMBER\",\"labels\":[\"test-reliability\"],\"reactions\":{\"total_count\":0,\"+1\":0,\"-1\":0,\"laugh\":0,\"confused\":0,\"heart\":0,\"hooray\":0,\"rocket\":0,\"eyes\":0},\"created_at\":\"2026-03-27T04:32:04Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-03-27T16:33:26Z\"}","type":"text"}]}}}
2026-03-30T10:37:34.8076347Z {"timestamp":"2026-03-30T10:33:55.236463703Z","direction":"OUT","type":"REQUEST","server_id":"github","method":"tools/call","payload":{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"tools/call","params":{"arguments":{"issue_number":37864,"method":"get","owner":"Azure","repo":"azure-sdk-for-js"},"name":"issue_read"}}}
2026-03-30T10:37:34.8096793Z {"timestamp":"2026-03-30T10:33:55.393706617Z","direction":"IN","type":"RESPONSE","server_id":"github","payload":{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"content":[{"text":"[{\"sha\":\"[REDACTED]\",\"html_url\":\"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]\",\"commit\":{\"message\":\"feat(warp): add resolveImports for #platform import resolution (#37818)\\n\\n## Motivation\\n\\nToday, warp handles platform-specific code (browser vs Node vs React\\nNative) via **`polyfillSuffix`** — a filename-convention trick where the\\ncompiler host secretly swaps `foo.ts` with `foo-browser.mts` at read\\ntime. This works for emit, but it **breaks TypeScript's type-checking\\nmodel**:\\n\\n- **TypeScript cannot see the real import graph.** The compiler thinks\\nit's reading `foo.ts` when it's actually compiling `foo-browser.mts`.\\nType errors in one platform variant can be silently masked.\\n- **No IDE support.** Go-to-definition, find-references, and rename\\ndon't understand the substitution — they always navigate to the default\\nfile, not the platform-specific one.\\n- **The indirection is invisible.** There's nothing in the source code\\nthat says \\\"this module has platform variants.\\\" You have to know the\\nnaming convention and check for sibling files.\\n\\nThis PR adds a **`resolveImports`** post-processing pipeline to warp\\nthat enables packages to use [Node.js subpath\\nimports](https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html#subpath-imports)\\n(`#platform/*`) instead. This is the foundation for migrating away from\\n`polyfillSuffix` entirely.\\n\\n## How it works\\n\\n### Before (polyfillSuffix)\\n\\n```\\nsrc/\\n  httpClient.ts              ← uses httpClientImpl directly\\n  httpClientImpl.ts          ← default implementation\\n  httpClientImpl-browser.mts ← browser variant (magic naming convention)\\n```\\n\\n```yaml\\n# warp.config.yml\\ntargets:\\n  - name: browser\\n    polyfillSuffix: -browser   # ← warp swaps file contents at compile time\\n```\\n\\nTypeScript sees `httpClient.ts` for all targets. The browser variant is\\ninvisible to the type system.\\n\\n### After (subpath imports)\\n\\n```\\nsrc/\\n  httpClient.ts              ← uses `import ... from \\\"#platform/httpClientImpl\\\"`\\n  httpClientImpl.ts          ← Node implementation (default)\\n  httpClientImpl-browser.mts ← browser implementation\\n```\\n\\n```jsonc\\n// package.json\\n{\\n  \\\"imports\\\": {\\n    \\\"#platform/*\\\": {\\n      \\\"browser\\\": \\\"./src/*-browser.mts\\\",\\n      \\\"react-native\\\": \\\"./src/*-browser.mts\\\",\\n      \\\"default\\\": \\\"./src/*.ts\\\"\\n    }\\n  }\\n}\\n```\\n\\n```jsonc\\n// src/tsconfig.browser.json\\n{\\n  \\\"compilerOptions\\\": {\\n    \\\"customConditions\\\": [\\\"browser\\\"]  // ← TS resolves #platform/* to browser files\\n  }\\n}\\n```\\n\\nNow TypeScript **resolves the platform choice itself** using standard\\nmodule resolution. The browser tsconfig sees browser types, the Node\\ntsconfig sees Node types. IDE navigation, rename, and find-references\\nall work correctly across platform boundaries.\\n\\nThe key shift: with `polyfillSuffix`, platform resolution is a\\n**build-tool trick** — only warp understands it. With `#platform/*`, it\\nbecomes a **standard TypeScript feature**. Any TS-aware tool — `tsc`,\\nyour IDE, API Extractor, ESLint — can independently resolve the correct\\nplatform files per tsconfig. Each platform variant can be type-checked\\n**without running warp at all**.\\n\\nWarp's new `resolveImports` step runs **after** compilation and rewrites\\n`#platform/*` specifiers in emitted `.js` and `.d.ts` files to concrete\\nrelative paths, so published packages don't require runtime `imports`\\nresolution.\\n\\n## What's in this PR\\n\\n- **`resolveImports.ts`** — Node.js-spec-compliant subpath import\\nresolver:\\n- Implements `PACKAGE_IMPORTS_EXPORTS_RESOLVE`,\\n`PACKAGE_TARGET_RESOLVE`, and `PATTERN_KEY_COMPARE` from the [Node.js\\nspec](https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html#resolution-algorithm-specification)\\n- Supports exact keys, wildcard patterns (`#platform/*.js`), condition\\nobjects, array fallbacks, and `null` targets\\n- Rewrites `import`/`export`/`require`/dynamic `import()` string\\nliterals via TypeScript AST\\n- Uses WeakMap caching for pattern key sorting (called hundreds of times\\nper target with the same imports map)\\n\\n- **`build.ts`** — Integrates `resolveImports` into the build pipeline:\\n- Automatically runs when `package.json` has an `imports` field (no\\nconfig needed)\\n- Builds the correct condition set per target (`customConditions` +\\n`import`/`require` + `default`)\\n  - Runs in parallel across targets\\n\\n- **927 lines of tests** covering:\\n- Unit tests for `resolveSubpathImport` (exact keys, patterns,\\nconditions, arrays, null, edge cases)\\n- Unit tests for `resolveImportsInContent` (ESM import/export, CJS\\nrequire, dynamic import, `.d.ts` declarations)\\n  - Integration tests that run full warp builds and verify output files\\n- Regression tests for spec edge cases (empty pattern matches, pattern\\nkey ordering)\\n\\n## Part of a series\\n\\nThis is the first of 4 stacked PRs:\\n\\n1. **This PR** — Add `resolveImports` to warp\\n2. `ts-http-runtime` platform refactor — migrate first package to\\n`#platform/*`\\n3. Remove `polyfillSu
